title: Building Sentences & Doing Overview
subtitle: Listen to the lesson explained in English

transcript.en: Welcome! 👋 Unit 6 — Building Sentences and Doing.
Four big things this unit: a **daily routine dialogue**, the **imperative** (giving commands), the **present tense**, and some key grammar — the future, saying 'I have', and 'there is'. Let's go!
☀️ PART 1 — MY DAY ☀️
We start with a mini dialogue about a typical day. Listen to how it flows.
Question one — what do you do in the morning?
And the answer — I get up early and drink a cappuccino:
Then — and after?
I go to work until six:
In the evening?
I eat at home and read a book:
Notice how each sentence uses a different verb — get up, drink, go, eat, read. All present tense. All built the same way.
📣 PART 2 — THE IMPERATIVE 📣
The imperative is the command form — telling one person or a group what to do.
Two forms: **singular** for one person, **plural** adds -u on the end.
Quick tip: drop the first letter of the present-tense verb to get the imperative. So 'I understand' in the present tense becomes:
Understand! Easy pattern. Now a few key commands. Go:
For a group, add -u. Come:
And the group form. Eat:
And the group form. Drink:
And the group form. Walk:
And the group form. Get up:
And the group form. Open:
And the group form. Close:
And the group form. Listen:
And the group form. 🎧
See the pattern in action — walk straight (one person):
Walk straight (a group):
Write your name (one person):
Write your name (a group):
The plural is almost always just: add -u. Simple! 🎯
🔁 PART 3 — PRESENT TENSE 🔁
The present tense is built from the imperative — just add a person-prefix at the front.
The prefix set: **n** = I, **t** = you or she, **j** = he. Plurals follow the same pattern.
If the imperative starts with a **vowel** — like the command for 'open' — just bolt the prefix on:
That's 'I open'. And:
You open — or she opens. And:
He opens. We open? Niftħu. They open? Jiftħu. 👍
If the imperative starts with a **consonant** — like the command for 'bring' — you add an 'i' buffer:
I bring. 'He brings' stays close to the root:
One of the most useful verbs: to eat. Listen to all three forms — I, you/she, he:
I eat.
You eat / she eats.
He eats. We eat and they eat follow the same pattern. 🍽️
Practise conjugating iekol in the exercises — once you crack one verb, the pattern works for all of them.
📖 PART 4 — GRAMMAR 📖
Four grammar points packed into this unit. Let's run through them quickly.
POINT 1: The future. Just add one tiny word in front of the present tense.
Use **se**, **ser**, or **ħa** — all mean 'going to'. For example:
I'm going to eat.
I'm going to write.
I'm going to sit down. One word. Future sorted. ✅
Important note: the future particle is **se** — never write 'sa' on its own for this. 'Sa' means 'until'.
POINT 2: Saying 'I have'. Maltese has a single dedicated verb for this.
I have time.
Do you have a car?
I don't have money. Notice the negative form — it wraps around the verb like a little bracket. 💡
POINT 3: 'There is / there are' — one single word covers both:
Examples:
There is a problem.
There are two chairs.
There's no time. The negative uses the same bracket trick you just heard. 🚫
POINT 4: Word order. The safe default is Subject-Verb-Object — just like English:
Joseph drinks the cappuccino.
Mary reads a book. Maltese can lead with the verb for emphasis — but Subject-Verb-Object always works. 👌
✅ DONE!
Daily routine. Commands. Present tense. Future, 'I have', 'there is'. That's Unit 6.
The sentence-building tools are all here. Use the exercises and watch it click. 💪
Saħħa! 🇲🇹
